coredata.dc:title: Fabrication of carbon quantum dots modified granular SnO2 nanotubes for visible light photocatalysis
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2016 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Pan, Jiaqi
Zhou, Yu
Cao, Jun
Sheng, Yingzhuo
Wu, Yan
Cui, Can
Li, Chaorong
Feng, Boxue
coredata.dc:description: The C QDs modified granular SnO2 nanotubes are prepared via a simple technical route through which SnO2 nanotubes are dispersed in C QDs solution, then dried at 80°C. The results of X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, Fourier-transform infra-red spectroscopy, transmission electron microscopy indicated that SnO2 nanotubes are well combined with C QDs through surface carbon–oxygen groups. By degradation of Rhodamine B under visible light irradiation, it demonstrates that the photocatalytic performance of these composites significantly enhanced. Furthermore, the unique up-converted photoluminescence behaviour of the carbon dots and the remarkable granular, tube-shape structure of the SnO2 are considered as the main reasons of this enhancement.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon materials
Nanocomposites
Luminescence
Functional

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The structure of C QDs modified SnO2 nanotubes composites: (a) SEM of C QDs modified SnO2 nanotubes, (b) XRD of C QDs modified SnO2 nanotubes, the composites coupled by C QDs solution of 1.5mg/mL is labelled as C/S 1.5, others similar, (c) TEM of C QD modified SnO2 nanotube, (d) HRTEM of C QD, (e) HRTEM of SnO2.
FT-IR and XPS spectra of C QDs modified SnO2 nanotubes. (a) FT-IR spectrum, (b)Full survey, (c) Sn 3d spectra. (d) C 1s spectra. (e) O 1s spectra.

(a) Photocatalytic degradation of different C QDs modified SnO2 nanotubes under visible light irradiation, (b) UV–visible absorption spectra of the samples, (c) Schematic illustration for the visible light photocatalytic process.

head.ce:title: Fabrication of carbon quantum dots modified granular SnO2 nanotubes for visible light photocatalysis

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics and Key Laboratory of ATMMT Ministry of Education Zhejiang Sci-Tech University, Hangzhou 310018, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Physics and Key Laboratory of ATMMT Ministry of Education Zhejiang Sci-Tech University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The C QDs modified granular SnO2 nanotubes are prepared via a simple technical route through which SnO2 nanotubes are dispersed in C QDs solution, then dried at 80°C. The results of X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, Fourier-transform infra-red spectroscopy, transmission electron microscopy indicated that SnO2 nanotubes are well combined with C QDs through surface carbon–oxygen groups. By degradation of Rhodamine B under visible light irradiation, it demonstrates that the photocatalytic performance of these composites significantly enhanced. Furthermore, the unique up-converted photoluminescence behaviour of the carbon dots and the remarkable granular, tube-shape structure of the SnO2 are considered as the main reasons of this enhancement.

ce:section.ce:para: With the development of the industry, the environmental pollution has attracted much more attention for lots of hazardous event, so how to degrade the pollution has became one of the extremely urgent problems. In plenty of ways, the catalytic degradation with the recyclability, energy saving and stability is regarded as the most promising choice. Especially the semiconductor photocatalyst, with the low cost and simple preparation enjoy great popularity. So far, lots of researches have been done, such as ZnO, TiO2, SrTiO3-based photocatalyst have been reported [1–4]. Even so, as known, on the surface of the earth where exist abundant visible light, but most of the photocatalyst are wide gap semiconductors, which means that the photocatalyst couldn’t work efficiently under sunlight. Therefore, how to improve the visible light utilization rate has been the focus. Now, lots of way have achieved, such as doping, recombination and modified, beside changing the band gap and improving the light utilization [4–7].

On the other hand, the carbon materials develop rapidly in recently years, in particular carbon quantum dots [8–11], have attracted the most attention since it was first reported in 2004 [12]. As our previously researches, beside the environmentally friendly, non-toxic properties and aqueous solubility, the C QDs shows the unique up converted photoluminescence behaviour, which may provide a new way to design the remarkable photocatalyst [13].

In this paper, the C QDs modified SnO2 nanotubes is prepared by direct coupling where the C QDs prepared via hydrothermal method and SnO2 nanotubes prepared via electrospinning. Further, the structure of the nanocomposites and the mechanism of enhancement of the visible light photocatalysis are studied.

First, the C QDs is prepared by the hydrothermal method [13] in 180°C and the granular SnO2 nanotubes [5] is prepared via a typical electrospinning and annealing in 700°C. Then the above products are coupled directly and dry at 80°C. (Detail view ESI).
The RhB degradation is used to evaluate the photocatalytic performance. (Detail view ESI).
The structures, crystallography, morphologies and spectrum of the samples are characterized by XRD, HRTEM, FESEM, XPS, UV–vis spectrophotometer, FTIR and spectro xsort. (Detail view ESI).

Fig. 1 is the morphology and structure characterization of the C QDs modified granular SnO2 nanotubes. Fig. 1a presents the SEM image of the samples, and the inset is the high resolution SEM of a single nanotube composite. As shown, the surface of the SnO2 nanotubes is granular and the average diameters of the nanotubes is about 200nm, which could benefit the adsorption of dye molecules onto nanotubes [14] and enhance the contact area between nanotubes and dye molecules. Limited by the resolution and size, the C QDs could not be seen. Fig. 1b is the XRD spectra of the SnO2 nanotubes with different C QDs. As revealed, the diffraction peaks are located in
2 θ =
26.6°, 34.0°, 37.9°, 39.1°, 51.8°, 54.8°, 57.9 °, 62.0°, 64.9°, 66.0°, 71.4°, 78.9°, which correspond to the diffractions of (110), (101), (200), (111), (211), (200), (002), (319), (112), (301), (202), (321) crystal planes[5] of tetragonal SnO2 (PDF#41-1445). There is no other diffraction peak, which indicates that the nanotubes composites have a highly crystalline SnO2 structure. While there is no obvious peak of carbon yet, that could be attributed to the small amounts and low crystallinity of it. However, as shown in the TEM images (Fig. 1c), it can be observed that the C QD is attached to the surfaces of the SnO2 nanotube. Fig. 1d and e show the HRTEM images of the C QD and SnO2 nanotube. The lattice spacing of 0.323nm corresponds to the (002) planes of C QD, and the lattice spacing of 0.334 is in good agreement with the (110) plane of SnO2 [13,15].

Fig. 2 is the FT-IR and XPS spectra of the samples. As shown in Fig. 2a, the peaks at 3429 and 1632cm−1 appearing in pure SnO2 nanotubes and C QDs modified SnO2 nanotubes belong to the vibrations of water absorbed onto the surface. The characteristic peak at 1372cm−1 appeared in the C QDs modified SnO2 nanotubes is attributed to epoxy C–O stretching vibration, which indicates that C QDs is successfully introduced into the nanotubes.

Further, XPS was carried out and Fig. 2b is the full survey spectrum. For detailed, as shown in Fig. 2c, the two peaks fitted at 486.5 and 495.0eV correspond to the Sn 3d5/2 and Sn 3d3/2 of SnO2, respectively. In Fig. 2d, the main peak at 284.6eV could be attributed to the C–C bond with a sp2 orbital. The peak at 286.0 and 288.3eV are assigned to the C–O and C=O bonds, respectively. Moreover, no peak of the Sn–C bond is found in both Fig. 2c and d, which indicates that the C QDs do not exist as a dopant in the composites. In Fig. 2e, the peaks at 529.8, 530.0 and 531.7eV are ascribed to Sn=O, C=O and C–OH. Both Fig. 2d and e indicate the existence of carbon–oxygen bonds in the C QDs modified SnO2 nanotubes composites.
From above, it could be proved that the C QDs have been introduced into the SnO2 nanotubes system through the formation of the C–O bond.

Fig. 3a shows the photocatalytic degradation of the C QDs modified SnO2 nanotubes with different amounts of C QDs under visible light irradiation. To eliminate the effect from physisorption and heating during irradiation, the beaker was stired in darkness for 30min and immersed in an ice–water mixture. As shown, there is no obvious photocatalysis in pure C QDs or pure SnO2 nanotubes. While it is interesting that the samples exhibit high photocatalysis after C QDs are introduced into the nanotubes. As seen, the photocatalytic efficiency increases gradually to 95% with the increasing concentration of C QDs from 0 to 1mg/mL, and then decrease in 1.5mg/mL. Based on this result, it is obvious that introducing suitable C QDs to SnO2 nanotubes could enhance the visible light photocatalytic activity efficiently. Therefore, it is significant to investigate the role of C QDs in this system.

The UV–visible absorption spectra of samples with different amounts of C QDs are shown in Fig. 3b. As seen, the pure SnO2 nanotubes exhibits weak absorption in the visible region, while all the C QDs modified SnO2 nanotubes exhibit obvious absorption in visible region, the absorption intensity increases with the increasing amount of C QDs and is considered as a main reasons for the enhancement of visible light photocatalysis. Further, the mechanism of the visible light photocatalysis should be provided.

As shown in Fig. 3c, in this system, the as-prepared C QDs with excellent up-converted photoluminescence (ESI Fig. S1) can convert visible light and near-infra-red light to shorter wavelengths, which is corresponded to previously reported [15–17]. In this design, after C QDs are introduced, a mass of visible light is converted to shorter wavelength, which could excite the SnO2 nanotubes to generate the photo-generated electron–hole pairs to enhance the photocatalytic activity efficiently and is considered as the most important role in this design. At the same time, C QDs display as an accepters to transfer the photo-generated electrons from semiconductor, which manifest that the C QDs can promote to separate the photo-generated electron–hole pairs [15,18–19] and is deemed as another important reason. It is interesting that there is an obvious decrease with excess amount of C QDs. Because the C QDs can absorb visible light though itself having no photocatalytic abilities [13] and limited by quantum yield, the excess C QDs can result in a competition for visible light absorption, which may lead the decreasing of visible light photocatalytisis.

On the other hand, the morphology is also an advantage for the photocatalysis. Benefiting from the granular and tube-shape [5], when the surfaces of the semiconductor SnO2 are irradiated with visible light, majority of photons is remained until completely absorbed, which could increase the solar energy utilization efficiently What’s more, the granular surface and tube-shape could increase the contact area and promote the convection of water to dilute the contaminants.
In light of the above advantages, the SnO2 nanotubes composites can efficiently degrade the Rh B solution and be expected to be a remarkable photocatalyst.

In this paper, the C QDs modified SnO2 nanotubes are prepared via a simple process and demonstrate excellent visible light photocatalytic activity. The mechanism of the enhancement is studied and the unique up-converted photoluminescence behaviour of the C QDs is considered as the most important reason. In addition, the C QDs can act as accepters to promote to separate the photo-generated electron–hole pairs and the granular and tube-shape structure are also considered as important reasons for remarkable photocatalystic activity. Such design may bring new insight to fabricate of excellent visible photocatalyst candidates and also could be applied in photoelectric field.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the support for this work from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant no. 61176005 and 51172209) and Science Foundation of Zhejiang Sci-Tech University (ZSTU) under Grant no. 15062091-Y and Zhejiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation under Grant no. LQ13A040006.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.matlet.2016.02.033.
